content.full_text:
2731 Pyron Street
Palo Alto, CA 94306
(415) 326-2388

May 18, 1990

Mr. Anthony C. Bennetti
Acting City Attorney
City of Palo Alto
250 Hamilton Avenue
Palo Alto, CA 94306

RE: Advice from the Fair Political Practices
Commission Regarding the Sand Hill Corridor
Joint Policy Committee; your letter of 4/25/90

Dear Mr. Bennetti:

Thank you for sending me a copy of your request to the
Fair Political Practices Commission for advice regarding the
Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee pursuant to the
provisions of the Political Reform Act.

As we discussed by telephone on April 30, 1990, I have
reviewed the materials you sent to the FPPC, and I believe
there are additional facts that should be considered by the
FPPC in this matter before they offer advice. Since our
telephone conversation, I have assembled materials from various
sources to document these additional facts.

Attached to this letter are the relevant documents.
I would appreciate it if you would review the attached material
and, if you agree with me that these facts are relevant, notify
the FPPC that they should consider these facts before taking
action in this matter.

Due to the time constraints involved and the desirability
of all parties to avoid any unnecessary delay, I am today sending
a copy of this letter and its attachments to the FPPC, so that
if you decide that the facts should be included as part of your
advice request, some time might be saved that would otherwise
be used to copy and mail these materials.

I understand that the FPPC cannot consider the materials
they receive from me until you include them as part of your
request for advice, because I am a third party in this matter.

If you have any questions, please contact me.

Sincerely,

Herb Porock

Attachments

cc: Fair Political Practices Commission

Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee

Page 1 of 5

The Committee is Governmental in Character

In a opinion requested by Samuel Siegel, City Attorney
of Pico Rivera, 3 PPPC Opinions 62 (No. 76-054, July 6, 1977)
the PPPC enumerated criteria for determining whether an entity
is governmental in character.

The first criterion is whether the impetus for the formation
of the entity was with a governmental body. The Sand Hill
Corridor Joint Policy Committee was formed when the City of
Palo Alto voted to "approve the Charter for the formation and
operation of the Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee."
See Attachment 1, Palo Alto city staff report OMR:395:7, August
13, 1987, and Attachment 2, Palo Alto City Council minutes
excerpt, August 17, 1987, Volume 58, pages 184 and 191.

The second criterion is whether all or most of the entity's
funds are received from public sources. The City of Palo Alto
provides "logistical staff support" for the Sand Hill Corridor
Joint Policy Committee. See Attachment 3, Charter of the committee.
Also, the City of Palo Alto provides meeting space and prints
all documents considered by the committee and/or forwarded to
the City Council.

The third criterion is whether the entity is performing
a function which public agencies are legally authorized to perform
or which they traditionally have performed. The Sand Hill Corridor
Joint Policy Committee serves "as a liaison to the governing
body of the respective jurisdictions", which is a function that
some public agencies legally and traditionally perform; identifies
"policy level decisions that should be referred to the governing
bodies for acceptance", which is a function that public agencies,
such as Palo Alto's Policies and Procedures Committee, legally
and traditionally perform; "provides a forum for the presentation
of views from the public", which is a function that public agencies,
such as City Councils and City committees, legally and traditionally
perform; and "reviews and recommends financing plans", which
is a function public agencies, such as Palo Alto's Finance and
Public Works Committee, legally and traditionally perform.
See Attachment 3, Charter of the Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy
Committee.

The last criterion is whether the entity is treated as "public"
by other statutory provisions. The Sand Hill Corridor Joint
Policy Committee is "subject to the applicable open meeting
statutes" (the Ralph M. Brown Act). See Attachment 1, Palo
Alto city staff report OMR:395:7, August 13, 1987, and Attachment 4,
Palo Alto City Council minutes excerpt, August 17, 1987, Volume
58, pages 178 and 179.

Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee

Page 2 of 5

The Committee May Make a Final Governmental Decision

Section 18700 (a)(1)(A) states that "A board or commission
possesses decision-making authority whenever: it may make a
final governmental decision". On December 11, 1989, the Palo
Alto City Council adopted the schedule contained in city staff
report CMR:56:9, November 30, 1989, that gave the Sand Hill
Corridor Joint Policy Committee decision-making authority to
determine the consultants to be interviewed and to interview
the consultants selected as part of the process used to choose
a consultant for the Sand Hill Corridor Draft Environmental
Impact Report. See Attachment 5, CMR:56:9, page 5, and City
Council "Action" agenda, December 4, 1989, pages 4 and 5 (labeled
as Attachment 6).

The Committee May Prevent a Governmental Decision

Section 18700 (a)(1)(B) states that "A board or commission
possesses decision-making authority whenever: ... it may prevent
a governmental decision either by an exclusive power to initiate
the decision or by reason of a veto which may not be overridden."
Palo Alto City Manager Zaner stated that moving the issue of
scoping for the Environmental Impact Report from the Sand Hill
Corridor Joint Policy Committee to the Planning Commission "would
in all likelihood require going back through the JPC to get
buy-in from the other agencies if changes were made". See
Attachment 6A, Palo Alto City Council minutes excerpt, March
13, 1989, Volume 61, page 188.

The Committee Makes Substantive Recommendations that are Approved

Section 18700 (a)(1)(C) states that "A board or commission
possesses decision-making authority whenever: it makes substantive
recommendations which are, and over an extended period of time
have been, regularly approved without significant amendment or
modification by another public official or governmental agency."
The Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy committee makes such recommendations, they are approved regularly by the Palo Alto City Council
without significant amendment. Sometimes the committee's actions
are presented as recommendations of city staff, but when they
are so presented, the city staff, who are public officials, are
approving the committee's recommendations without significant
amendment. On occasion, the same recommendation is presented
as both a recommendation of the committee and staff, or recorded
at one place as a committee recommendation and at another place
as a staff recommendation.

The committee's Charter (Attachment 3) states that the
purposes of the Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy committee include
the following: "recommend to their respective jurisdictions
whether to proceed as called for in the 'Framework' and "recommend
financing plans for the project".

Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee

Page 3 of 5


Meeting agendas for the Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy
Committee for March 23, 1989 (Attachment 7) and January 24, 1990
(Attachment 8) include agenda items to "consider recommendation
on incorporating Traffic Studies into Part B of Procedural Frame-
work" (March 29, 1989) and "JPC recommendation concerning
consultant selection for the Sand Hill Corridor Environmental
Document" (January 24, 1990).

Acting City Attorney Anthony C. Bennelli's request for advice
to the PPPC of April 23, 1990, states that "the Committee ...
is ready to make a recommendation to the City Councils of Palo
Alto and Menlo Park..." See Attachment 9, page 3 of letter.

The Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee on November
12, 1987, "unanimously adopted a work plan for preparation of
the Procedural Framework" that was adopted without significant
amendment by City Manager William Zaner, a public official, and
by the City Council. See Attachment 10, city staff report CHR:100:8,
December 31, 1987, and Palo Alto City Council minutes excerpt,
January 4, 1988, Volume 59, pages 114 and 115 (Attachment 11).

The Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee on May 16,
1988, "unanimously authorized City of Palo Alto staff to negotiate"
a contract with CH2MHill, Inc. for the Sand Hill Corridor Procedural
Framework, which recommendation staff agreed to without significant
amendment. See Attachment 12, city staff report CHR:329:8,
first page. The City Council approved the contract staff had been
negotiating, thereby tacitly accepting the recommendation made
by the committee as to which consultant should be selected for
contract negotiations. See also Attachment 13, under heading
"Next Steps" of city staff report SNDHIT/GZ, 5/12/88, page 3.

The Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee made additional
recommendations to the Palo Alto City Council in March 1989 that
were approved without significant amendment. These recommendations
are contained in Attachment 14, city staff report OMR:174:9, pp. 2,3,
March 9, 1989, and the City Council's action is shown in both
Attachment 15, City Council "Action" agenda, March 13, 1989,
pages 3 and 4, and Attachment 16, City Council minutes excerpt,
March 13, 1989, Volume 61, pages 185, 189, and 190.

The Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee again made
recommendations to the Palo Alto City Council in April 1989
that were approved without significant amendment. See Attachment 17,
City Council "Action" agenda, April 10, 1989, pages 5 and 6, and
Attachment 18, City Council minutes excerpt, April 10, 1989,
Volume 61, pages 273 and 275.

There has never been an incident where either the staff
or the City Council has rejected or significantly amended a
recommendation of the Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee.

Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee

Page 4 of 5

Non-Elected Members of Committee are Public Officials

The Stanford University employees who are members of the
Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee are "members" of a
local government agency within the meaning of Section 18700
even if they do not possess decision-making authority as described
in subsections (a)(1)(A), (B), and (C).

In an opinion requested by Doreet Rotman, Harriet Breyer,
Tom Bush, Brian Moore, and Father William Thom, 10 FPPC Opinion 1
(No. 86-001, May 12, 1987) the FPPC reached similar conclusions
by the following reasoning:

"Regulation 18700(a)(1) provides that the term "member"
is not limited to members of boards or commissions with decision-
making authority. ...

"Section 81001(b) provides: (b) Public officials, whether
elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them;

"Section 81002(c) provides: (c) Assets and income of public
officials which may be materially affected by their official
actions should be disclosed and in appropriate circumstances
the officials should be disqualified from acting in order that
conflicts of interest may be avoided.

"Section 81003 provides: This title should be liberally
construed to accomplish its purposes." (10 FPPC 7, emphasis
in the original)

The FPPC went on to note that "disqualification is required
only if it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have
a material financial effect distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on the official's economic interest."
(10 FPPC 8, emphasis in the original)

The Committee Members Vote

Section 18700(b) states that "A public official 'makes a
governmental decision' ... when he or she, acting within the
authority of his or her office: (1) Votes on a matter; ..."

The term "votes on a matter" is not further defined as to
its formality or informality.

A similar lack of definition existed in the Brown Act (the
open meeting law) prior to an appellate court decision in the
matter of Adler v. Culver City, where the court interpreted
the then existing Brown Act very narrowly to restrict its
application to formal meetings and actions.

Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee

Page 5 of 5

In 1961, the State legislature, in response to the decision
in Adler v. Culver City, enacted new legislation which appears
as Section 54952.c: Actions taken, definition.

"As used in this chapter, "action taken" means a collective
decision made by a majority of the members of a legislative body,
a collective commitment or promise by a majority of the members
of a legislative body to make a positive or a negative decision,
or an actual vote by a majority of the members of a legislative
body when sitting as a body or entity, upon a motion, proposal,
resolution, order or ordinance."

Material Effect of Non-elected Members Decisions is Distinguishable

The effect of the decisions of the Sand Hill Corridor Joint
Policy Committee on the economic interests of the committee
members who are Stanford University employees is distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, because "Stanford
University and Stanford Medical Center will be the primary
beneficiaries of the proposed Sand Hill Corridor project". See
Attachment 19, draft ordinance, attachment to city staff report
CMR:216:9, April 6, 1989.

One of the two Stanford University employees on the Sand
Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee is the university's vice
president for administrative resources, who was authorized by
the Board of Trustees of Stanford University to spend money
on behalf of the trustees for improvements in the Sand Hill
Corridor. See Attachment 20, Board of Trustees Report, item
27, from university publication Campus Report, July 6, 1989.

This committee member also signed the Sand Hill Corridor
Study Agreement with the City of Palo Alto on behalf of the
university. See Attachment 21, Sand Hill Corridor Study Agreement,
page 5 of 5. (Palo Alto Miscellaneous Document 682)

OFFICE OF

CITY ATTORNEY
(415) 329-2171

FAX No.:
,CITY OF PALO ALTO

(415) 329-2646

P. O BOX 10250
PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94303

750 HAMILTON AVENUE
PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94301

August 24, 1990

Blanca Breeze, Counsel
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

RE: Our Letter No. 90-292
Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee

Dear Ms. Breeze:

This letter follows the former Acting City Attorney's
April 23, 1990, request for advice regarding the above-referenced
matter. By letter dated May 1, 1990, the Commission's General
Counsel acknowledged receipt of our advice request and indicated
that you would be handling this matter. Thereafter, by letter
dated May 18, 1990, a Mr. Herb Borock, a resident of Palo Alto,
submitted to the Acting City Attorney an additional packet of
information he believed to be pertinent to your inquiry. Mr.
Borock apparently sent a copy of that material to the Commission as
well.

I have reviewed the material Mr. Borock submitted. Apart
from his covering letter, which consists of various legal
arguments, and an attachment consisting of a newspaper article, the
materials are generally excerpts from minutes, staff reports and/or
draft ordinances which relate to the Sand Hill Corridor Joint
Policy Committee matter. As we discussed this afternoon, I believe
the materials are accurate, but they are also incomplete.
Accordingly, I have attached Mr. Borock's submittal, without his
cover letter or the news clippings, so that you may take these
materials into account in preparing your advice.

I would, however, like to supplement these materials with
a chronology of the Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee's
operations and a more complete package of the relevant minutes,
staff reports and other materials. I will enlist the assistance of
our City Clerk's office in this matter, and expect to be able to
forward these additional materials to you next week. Obviously, it
is essential that you have a complete description of the actual
background and context of the situation before rendering any
advice.

900824 kp 0050008

Ms. Blanca Breeze
Page Two
August 24, 1990

RE:  Our Letter No. 90-292
     Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee

I hope the foregoing approach will be of assistance to
your task.  If you would like any other information, or if I can be
of any further assistance if any way, please do not hesitate to
contact me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        ARIEL PIERRE CALONNE
                                        City Attorney

APC:kp

cc:  City Council
     Marvin Overway
     Sue Case

Enclosure




900824 kp 0050008

OFFICE OF
CITY ATTORNEY
(415) 329-2171

CITY OF PALO ALTO

FAX No.:
(415) 329-2646

P.O. BOX 10250
PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94303

250 HAMILTON AVENUE
PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94301

August 31, 1990

Blanca Breeze, Counsel
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

RE: Our Letter No. 90-292
Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee

Dear Ms. Breeze:

This letter follows my transmittal of August 24, 1990,
regarding the above-referenced advice request. Enclosed please
find what I believe to be a complete package of the relevant
minutes and staff reports from the Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy
Committee and Palo Alto City Council. You will also find brief
chronological listings of the relevant meeting dates. I believe
these materials will provide you with sufficient information to
complete your consideration of our advice request. I would note,
however, that we have not included any of the Menlo Park City
Council materials.

As I understand the question presented, the primary focus
of your review will be whether the Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy
Committee possesses decision making authority so that the Committee
participants constitute "members" within the meaning of section
18700(a)(1) of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. In
particular, I understand the question to be whether the Joint
Policy Committee:

" . . . makes substantive recommendations
which are, and over an extended period of time
have been, regularly approved without
significant amendment or modification by
another public official or governmental
agency." (Cal. Code Regs., tit. 2, §
18700(a)(1)(C).)

I would submit the following commentary for your
assistance in interpreting these materials. First, you should be
aware that the Joint Policy Committee partially fulfills the early
public consultation provisions of the California Environmental
Quality Act, Public Resources Code sections 21000, et seq. and the

900831 kp 0050018

Ms. Blanca Breeze
August 31, 1990
Page 2

RE: Our Letter No. 90-292
Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee

State CEQA Guidelines, California Code of Regulations, title 14,
sections 15000, et seq. As you may be aware, "scoping" the
environmental review process for a major project helps public
agencies identify the range of actions, alternatives, mitigation
measures and significant effects to be analyzed in an EIR. (State
CEQA Guidelines, § 15083.) The only substantive issue underlying
the Joint Policy Committee's information -- how and whether the Sand
Hill Road Corridor might be improved -- has been the subject of
previous litigation which successfully attacked the adequacy of an
EIR prepared by the City of Palo Alto. Accordingly, the public and
private decision makers involved at the present time are
particularly sensitive to the important state policies calling for
public participation in the environmental review process. (See,
e.g., State CEQA Guidelines, §§ 15003 and 15006.) The Joint Policy
Committee thus represents an effort to fulfill the letter and
spirit of CEQA's public consultation goals.

I would also observe that the Joint Policy Committee is
bound by its "charter" to be, and in fact has performed as, a
procedural review body. Indeed, the Committee's basic task was to
review the "Procedural Framework for the Sand Hill Corridor"
project. Likewise, you will find, without significant exception,
each of the relevant Joint Policy Committee staff reports to be
focused upon implementing the procedural issues associated with the
project. The Committee's limited powers raise, in my view, serious
doubt as to whether the Committee could ever make "substantive
recommendations" within the meaning of the Fair Political Practices
Commission's regulations.

I would also direct your attention to the Palo Alto City
Council meeting minutes accompanying this letter. This extensive
compilation of minutes represents hour upon hour of City Council
deliberation, review and action upon issues forwarded to the
Council by the Joint Policy Committee. The Council's minutes
reveal several instances during which the City Council gave serious
consideration to amending and/or rejecting various recommendations
made by the Joint Policy Committee. For example, the Council's
December 4, 1989, minutes chronicle the meeting at which both the
Request for Proposal to Prospective EIR Consultants and the Study
Agreement with Stanford University were considered by City Council.
The Joint Policy Committee recommendation was significantly amended
to modify the EIR consultant RFP to include meetings with the Palo
Alto Bicycle Advisory Committee. At the same meeting, several
other amendments were proposed by Council Members and defeated.
These records unquestionably show detailed review and modification
of Joint Policy Committee recommendations to the City Council.

900831 kp 0050018

Mrs. Blanca Breeze
August 31, 1990
Page 3

RE: Our Letter No. 90-292
Sand Hill Corridor Joint Policy Committee

I hope the foregoing information and commentary is useful
to you. If you have any questions, or if I may be of any further
assistance, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

ARIEL PIERRE CALONNE
City Attorney

APC:kp

cc: Bill Zaner, City Manager

Enclosures

900831 kp 0050018
